evidence: README.md explicitly binds contract governance and quality-gate expectations to `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/startram/errors.go`.

`goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` contains explicit namespace-scoped action registries plus version-aware compatibility helpers (`IsUploadActionContractActive`, `IsC2CActionContractActive`, deprecation checks), which matches the documented intention of contract lifecycle governance.
`goseg/startram/errors.go` centralizes a masked API-facing error contract with explicit compatibility metadata and a version gate helper, reinforcing documented edge-contract governance.
Both contract files remain lightweight and domain-focused, but docs still describe quality gates at a repo-wide level without tying to package-level ownership boundaries beyond file names.
high_level_elegance.issues_preventing_higher_score: ['README does not document a concrete policy for contract evolution review cadence (e.g., required maintainer-facing checklist for version bumps/deprecations) even though it names specific contract files, which leaves governance expectations somewhat implicit for future contributors.', 'README references quality gates but does not explicitly state ownership of contract compatibility review outcomes (who approves version increments/compatibility status), creating a small process ambiguity in the governance layer.', 'Protocol contract documentation in README links to a single contract surface (actions/errors) without pointing to tests or changelog expectations for `C2C` contract changes, despite C2C being present in the protocol file.']

evidence: `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_seams.go` and `goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go` both introduce large closure-heavy runtime structs instead of cohesive interfaces, each test seam re-creating similar dependency concerns (state update, logging, config retrieval, subsystem orchestration).
`goseg/main.go` builds subsystems by wiring long lists of functions rather than passing narrow interface contracts, forcing call sites to know too much about implementation details.
The same dependency concepts (e.g., logging, config/runtime synchronization, external process hooks) are rewrapped across modules instead of being normalized behind shared abstractions.

`goseg/routines/version.go` triggers background sync in `startVersionDiscovery` via a detached goroutine that sends results through a channel without structured failure handling or lifecycle cancellation hooks from the caller.

`goseg/importer/importer.go` performs environment/drive resolution before completing token-based authorization checks, increasing failure surface and making side effects occur before the primary security guard.

Cross-module seams in `goseg/auth/auth.go` and `goseg/startup_orchestrator.go` are coupled by ad-hoc callback signatures rather than stable contracts, making it easy to compose inconsistent call patterns.
`goseg/broadcast/broadcast.go` starts goroutines in the broadcast loop while explicitly discarding returned errors from tick execution, so failures are silently dropped.
The same path has no bounded backoff or centralized stop-aware retry strategy when tick/update operations fail repeatedly.
`goseg/routines/version.go` has a fire-and-forget version-sync goroutine that reports only via channel value, not via explicit error propagation.

goseg/logger/logger.go mixes low-level log transport wiring (`FileWriter`, `makeLoggerCore`, `Initialize`) with unrelated runtime duties like session map orchestration (`SetSysLogSessions`, `SetDockerLogSessions`), user-visible startup side effects (`printStartupBanner`), and log artifact composition (`RetrieveSysLogHistory`, `getDockerLogs`).

Across `goseg/system/system.go`, `goseg/system/wifi.go`, and `goseg/system/disk.go`, package-wide mutable globals and host-level command execution are spread across multiple unrelated system concerns (HW telemetry, Wi-Fi lifecycle, filesystem provisioning) with no explicit boundary object.

`goseg/structs/ws.go` combines websocket transport DTOs for multiple domains (`WsUrbitPayload`, `WsSystemPayload`, `WsUploadPayload`, etc.) together with connection/session helpers and broadcast behavior, creating a shared “god” types/concern file that is edited for unrelated feature areas.

findings.summary: Distributed protocol compatibility definitions split ownership of version policy across packages.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` defines `ContractCompatibility` aliases and `Compatibility*` constants locally while `goseg/startram/errors.go` defines its own `ErrorContract*` constants for the same conceptual domain.
The two files independently carry `CurrentContractVersion`/`IntroducedIn` style versioning metadata for compatible contract behavior.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single canonical contract-compatibility module (for example in `goseg/protocol/contracts`) that exports compatibility enums and contract-version policy constants, then update `actions` and `startram` to import and reuse that surface instead of redefining parallel constants.

findings.summary: Hardcoded contract version literals are maintained separately, creating synchronization risk.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions.go` and `goseg/startram/errors.go` each embed concrete contract-version literals in contract descriptors/helpers rather than referencing one source of truth.
Both packages encode lifecycle checkpoints (`IntroducedIn`/current-version checks) independently, so a version rollout must be updated in multiple places manually.
findings.suggestion: Create a single package-level version constant (or generated version descriptor) and inject/derive contract metadata from it across protocol and startram, so contract checks all read from the same compatibility timeline.

findings.summary: Broadcast runtime drops tick errors, making background update failures invisible and unobservable.
evidence: `StartBroadcastLoopWithRuntime` launches async tick execution and the returned error is intentionally ignored, so update/transform failures never bubble to startup orchestration or observability.
No retry cap/backoff and no error channel means repeated transport/config errors cannot be surfaced or tripped into a controlled shutdown path.
findings.suggestion: Return tick errors through an explicit result channel or callback, add bounded retry/backoff with cancellation-aware loop termination, and fail fast or degrade gracefully when persistent errors are detected.

findings.summary: Runtime seam layer is fragmented into many function fields, reducing interface clarity and compounding adapter complexity.
evidence: Multiple modules independently define constructor-time function bags for overlapping concerns instead of reusable interfaces, forcing consumers to wire broad, manually-maintained dependency lists.
`main`-level bootstrap logic is coupled to module-specific callback shapes, which defeats stable module boundaries and increases coupling for tests and consumers.
New seams are added as ad-hoc closures without a consolidated contract for lifecycle, configuration, and system interactions.
findings.suggestion: Introduce small, cohesive interfaces for each dependency domain (e.g., broadcaster, version sync, ship/runtime ops) and compose them in a small adapter layer so modules consume stable contracts rather than hand-built closure maps.

findings.summary: Version-sync goroutine has no cancellation-aware error/exit channel contract, risking blocked sends and unbounded background work.
evidence: A spawned goroutine in version discovery sends sync output into a channel but error handling is limited to the success path and is not tied to caller cancellation.
The loop structure assumes a single happy-path value and does not expose an explicit failure signal separate from data updates.
findings.suggestion: Thread a context/cancel path through discovery, return both update and error via a structured channel payload, and ensure goroutine exits without blocking when background orchestration is stopped.

findings.summary: Importer performs resource selection before authorization validation, enabling avoidable work on unauthorized calls and inconsistent failure timing.
evidence: `OpenUploadEndpoint` resolves selected/custom drives before confirming the request is authorized.
This order can trigger IO and lookup side effects even when the caller fails auth, and pushes security checks late in the call path.
findings.suggestion: Reorder `OpenUploadEndpoint` to perform authentication/authorization first, then execute storage discovery and endpoint creation only for authorized sessions.

findings.summary: Config context has split ownership between legacy globals and a dedicated context struct.
evidence: `config.go` defines a protected `runtimeContext` plus exported globals `BasePath`, `Architecture`, `DebugMode`, and `DockerDir`.
`applyRuntimeContext` updates both `runtimeContext` and those globals, and `setRuntimeContext`/`initializePaths` rely on this synchronized-but-redundant path.
`Bootstrap` initialization reads `RuntimeContextSnapshot()` while other package consumers can still read/write the duplicate globals.
findings.suggestion: Collapse process execution context to a single canonical source (the runtime context object), remove or internalize legacy exported globals, and migrate call sites to explicit `RuntimeContext` injection/accessors to avoid split authority and brittle cross-file coupling.

findings.summary: Container config dispatch API accepts free-form strings despite existing typed container domain model.
evidence: `ContainerConfigForType` accepts `containerType string` and casts to `transition.ContainerType` before map lookup, so unknown values are only rejected at runtime.
The package already has a typed domain (`transition.ContainerType`) used for registration, but this internal clarity is not enforced at the public API boundary.
findings.suggestion: Make the public dispatch API use the typed `transition.ContainerType` directly (or an explicit domain wrapper), keep string conversion in one external adapter, and document the allowed values to keep boundary contracts explicit and compile-time safe.

findings.summary: Logger package blends initialization, session registry, transport formatting, and host-facing output into one surface.
evidence: Initialize does global logger lifecycle plus user output side effects (`printStartupBanner()`, `fmt.Printf` fallback messages) before handing off runtime behavior.

Same package also owns both infra initialization APIs (`Initialize`, `makeLoggerCore`, global `LogPath`/`loggerInitState`) and runtime log transport/session APIs (`SysLogSessions`, `AddSysLogSession`, `DockerLogSessions`, `GetDockerLogs`, `RetrieveSysLogHistory`).
Routine code in logstream directly mutates logger-owned session state, indicating blurred ownership boundaries (`logger.RemoveSysSessions`, `logger.SetDockerLogSessionLive`, etc.).
findings.suggestion: Split logging into two packages/modules: one for logger core setup/policy (initialization, sinks, level config), and one for log distribution contracts (stream session registry, websocket fan-out DTO formatting, history retrieval). Move banner/CLI-facing output and fallback UX text out of core logging init into a host/bootstrap layer.

findings.summary: System package mixes orthogonal responsibilities through package-level state and shell-driven host mutation.
evidence: System modules expose mutable process-local globals for runtime state (`Device`, `wifiInfoLoopStop`, `C2CStoredSSIDs`, `smartResults`, `runCommand`-style hooks), while also performing command execution and lifecycle orchestration.
`system.go` mixes host discovery/hardware introspection (`GetMemory`, `GetCPU`, `GetDisk`, `GetTemp`) with install/recovery behavior (`FixerScript`, `cronExists`, `addCron`).
`wifi.go` and `disk.go` similarly intermingle environment checks, stateful lifecycle control, shell/process manipulation, and file-system/disk provisioning concerns in same package-wide namespace.
findings.suggestion: Refactor into explicit sub-services (`system/hw`, `system/net`, `system/disk`, `system/orchestration`) each with injected command/run dependencies. Replace package-level mutable singletons with context-bound service structs so tests target behavior without mutating global state.

findings.summary: `structs` hosts unrelated websocket protocol domains and connection/session behaviors in one large cohesion-poor file.
evidence: A single file defines dozens of domain-specific payload/action structs (`WsUrbitPayload`, `WsSystemPayload`, `WsUploadPayload`, `WsSwapAction`, etc.) plus `ClientManager` behavior (`NewConnection`, `BroadcastAuth`, `CleanupStaleSessions`) and lock/snapshot helpers.
Multiple domains import these shared websocket types (`auth`, `leak`, ship handlers), so any change to one transport contract can affect unrelated feature ownerships.
The package is serving both data-schema and mutable transport state concerns, which increases coupling and makes responsibility boundaries unclear.
findings.suggestion: Split websocket contract definitions by feature domain (system/ship/leak/upload/c2c) and extract connection/session lifecycle into a dedicated package, then re-export only narrow, typed protocol contracts needed by handlers and dispatch layers.
